source: Chen & Ejov, multiple linear regression of 135 real-estate listings scraped from 7thheavenproperties.com (12 May 2020). Dependent variable: listed sale price (USD).
model: Multiple linear regression (log-transformed price), stepwise selection

coefficients.factor: Located on a hill (vs other locations)
coefficients.label: On a hill: -40.4%

coefficients.factor: Located on the South Coast (vs other locations)
coefficients.label: South Coast: -51.0%

notes: Total number of bed + bath rooms combined predicts price better than bedroom count alone.
Sample is uneven: St James dominates listings; St George has 1, St Thomas and St Philip 2 each; few 'plantation house' or 'estate' types.
Listed price (not final sale price) and missing floor-area data limit the model; spatial autocorrelation tests (GWR) are a recommended next step.

Market is segmented by buyer age/nationality: older European buyers favour larger West Coast villas; younger American/Caribbean buyers favour smaller South Coast homes closer to Bridgetown and the airport.
hypotheses_tested: Price rises with number of rooms (bedrooms + bathrooms).
Coastal / beach-access properties command the highest prices; the West (Platinum) Coast is most expensive.
Historically developed coastal parishes (St James, St Michael, Christ Church) cost more than rural parishes.
Gated properties cost more than non-gated.
Property type matters less than room count.
